Q1: Honestly, my study routine was chaotic at best. I'm 29, working part-time, and dealing with a lot of anxiety that makes it hard to sit down and focus consistently. I'd try to carve out time on weekends, but life kept getting in the way. Most weeks I'd end up cramming whenever I could find a spare moment, usually late at night when my anxiety was worst. I aimed for structure but rarely achieved it.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what crisis was happening that week. Some weeks I'd manage to study a bit each day, but then something would derail me—a work shift change, a panic attack, family stuff—and I'd fall behind again. Deadlines were the only thing that really forced me into action, which meant everything piled up at once. I wish I could say I had regular times, but that just wasn't my reality.
Q3: Usually panic prompted me to log in, if I'm being honest. I'd suddenly remember an assignment was due or realize I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd get email notifications that would trigger that sick feeling in my stomach, and I'd force myself to check what was happening. The gamified tasks actually helped a bit—earning points felt less overwhelming than facing a massive assignment.
Q4: There was a brief period early on where I tried to make logging in part of my morning routine with coffee. It worked for maybe two weeks before everything fell apart. The gamified elements kept me coming back more than anything else—there was something about the achievement badges that made it feel less like drowning in coursework. But I never really established a solid habit; my anxiety would spike and I'd avoid the LMS for days.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline because I'm a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and watch them when my brain was actually functioning. I watched over two hours of content, which doesn't sound like much, but for me that was significant effort. The gamified tasks also helped because they broke things into smaller, less terrifying pieces. Anything visual and bite-sized felt manageable.
Q6: I barely touched the readings and long text-based resources. My anxiety makes it hard to concentrate on dense text—the words just swim around. Discussion forums felt intimidating too; I'd read them but posting felt like exposing myself to judgment. I kept telling myself I'd get to the supplementary materials 'later,' but later never came. It wasn't that they weren't useful; I just couldn't handle them mentally.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate progress or when it didn't require too much mental energy to start. The gamified elements were brilliant for this—I could see my points accumulating, which gave me a tiny dopamine hit. Anything that felt achievable in one sitting was more likely to get done. If something required multiple steps or felt ambiguous, my brain would shut down before I even started.
Q8: Required activities felt like this heavy weight on my chest, while optional ones felt like they didn't exist at all. I couldn't even handle the required stuff most of the time, so optional activities were completely off my radar. It's strange—the pressure of requirements paralyzed me, but without that pressure, I had no motivation to engage. I needed something in between, which the gamified tasks kind of provided.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I'd download lecture slides and study them on my tablet away from the computer. Being on the LMS triggered my anxiety because I could see all the things I was behind on. Offline, I could focus on just one thing without the overwhelming dashboard of failure staring at me. I also used YouTube videos on similar topics when the course material wasn't clicking.
Q10: So many times. I'd download a PDF and spend hours going through it with highlighters and sticky notes, but the LMS would just show I accessed it once for two minutes. I'd rewatch downloaded videos multiple times while taking handwritten notes—my visual learning style means I need to see things repeatedly. The system couldn't capture me lying in bed at 2 AM, anxiously reviewing my notes and trying to make sense of everything.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a video or document once, download it, and then work with it offline for days. My internet at home isn't always reliable, and honestly, being disconnected from the LMS felt safer for my mental health. I could pretend I was just studying normally without confronting how far behind I was. The LMS would show minimal activity, but I was genuinely trying to learn the material.
Q12: There were several points where I just couldn't engage anymore. My anxiety would spike so high that even opening my laptop felt impossible. Around midterms, I had a particularly bad few weeks where work stress and course stress collided, and I basically disappeared. The attendance requirement felt impossible when some days I could barely get out of bed. My resilience is pretty low, so setbacks hit me harder than they might hit others.
Q13: Massively. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by competing responsibilities, the LMS became this thing I actively avoided. Confusion about assignments made my anxiety worse, which made me avoid the platform more, which made me more confused—it was a vicious cycle. Some weeks I'd log in, see how much I'd missed, and immediately log out because I couldn't handle the stress. Fatigue and anxiety together basically shut me down.
Q14: More times than I can count. I'd scroll through materials with glazed eyes, technically 'accessing' content but absorbing nothing. My brain would be too busy catastrophizing about failing to actually process what I was reading. Sometimes I'd watch an entire video and realize at the end that I hadn't retained a single thing. The engagement was performative—I was going through motions without actually learning.
Q15: I postponed almost everything important, which is why my assignment completion is so low despite my actual assignment scores being decent when I did submit. The anxiety creates this paralysis where the more important something is, the harder it is to start. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but that better day rarely came. Avoidance became my coping mechanism, even though I knew it was making things worse.
Q16: Deadlines were simultaneously my enemy and my only motivator. They'd create panic that sometimes pushed me into action, but often the panic was so overwhelming that I'd freeze instead. I'd log in frantically as deadlines approached, trying to cram everything in at once. The 20-hour average response time probably reflects all those late-night panic sessions. Deadlines didn't help me pace myself; they just concentrated all my stress into horrible bursts.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it affected me deeply—sometimes too deeply. Positive feedback gave me a brief boost, but negative feedback could send me spiraling for days. I'd avoid checking my grades because I was terrified of confirmation that I was failing. The gamified feedback was easier to handle because it felt less personal, more like a game score than a judgment of my worth as a student.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming from the start. There was so much content, so many deadlines, and I couldn't figure out how to prioritize. The gamified tasks were the one structural element that worked for me because they broke things down. But overall, the structure assumed a level of consistent engagement that I just couldn't maintain. I needed more flexibility and smaller milestones.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier—they gave me achievable goals and visible progress. But the sheer volume of content and the rigid attendance expectations made things harder. As someone with high anxiety, I needed more scaffolding and check-ins, not just content dumps. The visual resources were helpful, but they were buried among text-heavy materials that I couldn't process. A more visual-first design would have helped me tremendously.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. I was logging in, watching videos, participating in discussions sometimes, but my assignment completion was terrible. The anxiety makes starting assignments feel impossible even when I'm engaging with the content. Someone might understand the material but be paralyzed when it comes to producing work. High activity can mean genuine effort that just doesn't translate into submissions.
Q21: That's definitely not my experience—I had relatively low activity AND performed poorly. But I can see how someone with better mental health and study skills could be efficient, learn quickly offline, and not need to spend hours on the LMS. Some people just process information faster or have better external resources. The LMS isn't the only place learning happens, so low activity doesn't necessarily mean low engagement with the actual content.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and what's happening offline. They can't capture me crying over my notes at midnight or the hours I spent with downloaded materials. They also can't show why someone isn't engaging—whether it's laziness, anxiety, life circumstances, or something else entirely. The data is just behavior without context.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and making genuine effort, even when it's hard. For me, it's those moments when I'm truly focused and absorbing information, not just going through motions. It's feeling connected to the content and motivated to understand it. But engagement also means showing up mentally, which is something I struggled with constantly. True engagement requires both presence and effort, and my anxiety often robbed me of both.
Q24: The LMS data would never show how much I actually wanted to succeed or how hard I was fighting against my own brain. It can't capture the panic attacks before logging in, the tears over assignments I couldn't start, or the genuine learning that happened offline. It wouldn't show that my 'happy' moments were often relief at small accomplishments rather than true happiness. My struggle isn't visible in clicks and timestamps—it's in the invisible battle between wanting to learn and being paralyzed by anxiety.
